label: Baroque & Rococo (1700–1749)
summary: The early 18th century was dominated by the opulent Baroque style transitioning into the lighter Rococo aesthetic. Women's fashion featured wide panniers (side hoops), heavily embroidered silk gowns, and elaborate powdered wigs. Men wore justaucorps coats with knee breeches, waistcoats, and tricorne hats. The French court at Versailles set the global standard for aristocratic dress.
prints: Toile de Jouy (printed linen with pastoral scenes)

label: Neoclassical (1750–1799)
summary: The late 18th century saw fashion evolve dramatically — from the extravagant heights of Rococo with its towering powdered wigs and wide hoops, to the cleaner neoclassical lines inspired by Greco-Roman antiquity following the French Revolution. By the 1780s–90s, silhouettes slimmed and simplified as revolutionary politics rejected aristocratic excess. Muslin and natural fabrics gained favor over heavy silk brocades.
brands: London tailoring houses (non-Savile Row established firms)
key_garments: Chemise à la Reine (Marie Antoinette's muslin gown)

label: Empire & Regency (1800–1824)
summary: The Regency and Empire period defined early 19th century fashion. High-waisted Empire silhouettes in fine white muslin dominated women's fashion, inspired by Greco-Roman sculpture. Short puffed sleeves, low necklines, and long columnar skirts created an ethereal look. Napoleon's court introduced more color and luxury trim. Men's fashion was shaped by Beau Brummell's understated elegance — perfectly tailored dark coats, tight pantaloons, and impeccable linen.
brands: Indian cotton merchants (East India Company suppliers)

label: Romantic Era (1825–1849)
summary: The Romantic Era brought exaggerated silhouettes — wide gigot (leg-of-mutton) sleeves, nipped waists, and full skirts supported by multiple petticoats. Fashion became increasingly elaborate with heavy trimmings, ribbons, and lace. By the 1840s sleeves slimmed and skirts grew wider still, anticipating the crinoline era. Romanticism in art and literature directly influenced fashion's taste for drama, historical references, and emotional expression.
aesthetics: Literary fashion (inspired by novels and poetry)
key_garments: Morning dress (day gown with high neckline)

label: Crinoline Era (1850–1874)
summary: The crinoline era (1850s–1860s) saw skirts reach their maximum width, supported by steel cage crinolines replacing heavy petticoats. Charles Frederick Worth established haute couture in Paris, dressing Empress Eugénie. The late 1860s brought the transition to the bustle silhouette — skirt fullness moved to the back. The Franco-Prussian War and shifting social values began to moderate the most extreme fashions.
brands: Liberty of London (from 1875, but emerging influence)
key_garments: Morning dress (day wear with high neckline)

label: Gilded Age (1875–1899)
summary: The Gilded Age and late Victorian era were defined by the bustle — rear fullness that created a dramatic S-curve profile. The first bustle era (1870s) gave way to the natural form briefly in the early 1880s, then a second bustle revival (1883–1889) with shelf-like rear extensions. The 1890s brought the New Woman aesthetic: tailored suits, bicycle-friendly divided skirts, and enormous leg-of-mutton sleeves returning. Aesthetic Dress reformers began advocating looser, artistic garments.

label: Edwardian (1900s)
summary: The Edwardian era opened the 20th century with the S-bend corset creating a dramatic posture — chest forward, hips back. Enormous picture hats, abundant lace, and pastel florals defined the Belle Époque aesthetic. Paul Poiret began challenging the corset's dominance by mid-decade. Gibson Girl imagery — the ideal of an independent, athletic woman — influenced fashion toward higher necklines and tailored shirtwaist blouses.

label: WWI Era (1910s)
summary: The 1910s brought radical change — Paul Poiret liberated women from the corset with hobble skirts and harem pants, inspired by Ballets Russes orientalism. By 1915–1919, WWI's practicalities shortened hems above the ankle, broadened shoulders, and introduced women to trousers for war work. The wartime silhouette was simpler, darker, and more utilitarian. Art Deco influences began emerging by decade's end.
silhouettes: High Empire waist with dropped hip

label: Roaring Twenties (1920s)
summary: The Roaring Twenties revolutionized fashion with the flapper aesthetic — dropped waists, short hemlines (just below the knee), bobbed hair, and a boyish silhouette that rejected Victorian curves. Art Deco geometry influenced fabric patterns and jewelry. Coco Chanel popularized jersey knit, the little black dress, and sportswear-inspired ease. Elsa Schiaparelli began her surrealist experiments. Prohibition-era jazz culture drove demand for glamorous eveningwear.

label: Art Deco (1930s)
summary: The Great Depression brought an elegant, sophisticated response — the bias-cut gown. Madeleine Vionnet perfected the technique that clung to the body in fluid waves. Hemlines dropped to the floor for evening wear, and Hollywood became the new Paris for fashion inspiration. Jean Harlow, Marlene Dietrich, and Greta Garbo defined glamour. Shoulders broadened, and practical sportswear and resort wear expanded. Elsa Schiaparelli's Surrealism added avant-garde wit.

label: Wartime & New Look (1940s)
summary: WWII austerity defined wartime fashion — utility suits, shorter hemlines to save fabric, broad square shoulders, and rationed everything. Women in the workforce adopted practical, no-nonsense dress. After 1947, Christian Dior's New Look shockingly reversed all wartime restrictions with billowing full skirts, nipped waists, and rounded shoulders — a fantasy of femininity that split opinion but swept the world. American sportswear by Claire McCardell offered a democratic alternative.

label: Atomic Age (1950s)
summary: The 1950s were fashion's golden age of feminine dressing — full circle skirts, sweater sets, pencil skirts, and poodle skirts defined the decade. The hourglass silhouette reigned with nipped waists and full hips or straight pencil silhouettes. Postwar optimism brought pastel color stories, fun novelty prints, and a new teenage consumer market. James Dean and Marlon Brando popularized blue jeans and leather jackets as rebellious youth fashion.

label: Kennedy Era (Early 1960s)
summary: The early 1960s carried 1950s elegance into a more modern, streamlined aesthetic. Jackie Kennedy's influence was enormous — Oleg Cassini's pill-box hats, boxy pastel suits, and white gloves defined refined American style. London's Mod scene began to emerge with geometric shapes and bold graphics. Youth culture was increasingly setting trends rather than following haute couture.

label: Swinging Sixties (Late 1960s)
summary: The late 1960s exploded with revolutionary fashion. The miniskirt (Mary Quant, Courrèges) became a symbol of sexual liberation. Mod fashion brought bold geometric patterns, Op Art graphics, and space-age plastics. Simultaneously, the counterculture hippie movement rejected mainstream fashion with peasant blouses, bell bottoms, tie-dye, and ethnic textiles. Yves Saint Laurent introduced Le Smoking tuxedo suit for women and Pop Art-inspired Mondrian dresses.

label: Boho & Glam Rock (Early 1970s)
summary: The early 1970s saw hippie culture at peak mainstream expression alongside emerging glam rock. Boho earth tones, peasant-inspired maxi skirts, and bell bottoms dominated. Crochet, macramé, and natural fabrics spoke to ecological consciousness. Glam rock introduced sequins, platform boots, and androgynous glamour via David Bowie and T. Rex. Hot pants emerged as a provocative counterpoint to the midi/maxi debate.

early 1970s hot pants fashion women vintage

label: Disco & Punk (Late 1970s)
summary: The late 1970s diverged dramatically — Studio 54 disco culture brought sequins, halter tops, and skin-baring glamour, while punk rock exploded in London with ripped clothing, safety pins, and anarchic DIY fashion from Vivienne Westwood. Halston and Calvin Klein defined American sportswear chic with minimalist luxury. The wrap dress by Diane von Fürstenberg became a symbol of working women's liberation. Silhouettes slimmed from the wide bell bottoms toward straighter cuts.

label: Power Dressing (Early 1980s)
summary: The early 1980s brought excess and power dressing — extreme shoulder pads, Dynasty-level glamour, and the 'dress for success' working woman suit. New Wave and New Romantic movements brought androgynous makeup, ruffles, and theatrical costume-inspired fashion. Japanese designers Comme des Garçons and Yohji Yamamoto shocked the fashion establishment in Paris with deconstructed, all-black intellectual fashion. MTV's launch in 1981 made music video style a major fashion driver.

label: Neon & Excess (Late 1980s)
summary: The late 1980s turned up the volume on everything — even bigger shoulders, louder colors, and more conspicuous consumption. Wall Street money culture and Reagan-era excess drove fashion to extremes. Hip-hop fashion emerged from New York with tracksuits, gold chains, and luxury logos. Acid wash jeans, neon colors, and aerobics wear spilled into everyday fashion. Designers like Versace, Lacroix, and Westwood competed in extravagance. The supermodel era began with Cindy Crawford, Naomi Campbell, and Linda Evangelista.

late 1980s Dynasty supermodel glamour fashion

label: Grunge Era (Early 1990s)
summary: The early 1990s reacted against 1980s excess with grunge and minimalism. Seattle's grunge scene brought flannel shirts, ripped jeans, combat boots, and thrift-store layering. Simultaneously, minimalist designers like Calvin Klein, Helmut Lang, and Jil Sander stripped fashion back to clean, unembellished lines. Hip-hop fashion grew more prominent with oversized silhouettes, baggy jeans, and sportswear. Supermodels ruled the catwalks and campaigns.
prints: Grunge band graphic (Nirvana, Pearl Jam, Soundgarden)

label: Y2K Transition (Late 1990s)
summary: The late 1990s balanced minimalism with glitzy pop culture — the Spice Girls, the rise of MTV TRL, and Sex and the City defined the era. Low-rise jeans, crop tops, baby tees, and platform shoes were everywhere. Logomania began its rise with Fendi baguettes and Gucci by Tom Ford. Rave culture influenced casual fashion with shiny fabrics and futuristic details. The fashion-forward Paris couture scene saw Alexander McQueen and John Galliano challenge traditional codes.

label: Y2K Maximalism (Early 2000s)
summary: Y2K fashion hit its full stride — ultra-low-rise jeans, rhinestone everything, velour tracksuits (Juicy Couture), cargo pants, and butterfly tops defined mall culture. Paris Hilton and Destiny's Child aesthetic dominated. Logomania peaked with visible Dior, Louis Vuitton, and Burberry logos. The boho-chic trend emerged with Nicole Richie and Kate Moss in peasant blouses and suede boots. Indie music influenced vintage-thrift layering.

label: Boho-Chic (Late 2000s)
summary: The late 2000s moved away from Y2K excess toward a new boho-chic influenced by festival culture, and then toward the first waves of normcore and recession-era restraint. Kate Moss at Glastonbury defined bohemian festival fashion. The Great Recession of 2008-09 shifted tastes toward understated luxury and quiet classics. Jeggings, skinny jeans, and the balenciaga motorcycle bag became iconic. The rise of fashion blogs began changing the industry.

label: Normcore (Early 2010s)
summary: The early 2010s saw normcore and androgynous dressing rise, fueled by Tumblr aesthetics and indie music. Instagram launched in 2010, permanently changing how fashion trends spread. High-waisted jeans returned, flannel shirts came back post-grunge, and the 'hipster' aesthetic of artisanal craftsmanship influenced fashion. Athleisure began its trajectory. Street style photographers at Fashion Weeks became stars. The color-blocked clean aesthetic contrasted with maximalist prints.

2011 2012 athleisure Lululemon fashion aesthetic

label: Streetwear Era (Late 2010s)
summary: The late 2010s were defined by social media aesthetics — Instagram-driven 'clean girl', streetwear's rise to high fashion, and the athleisure explosion. Virgil Abloh's Off-White and Demna Gvasalia's Vetements deconstructed fashion's hierarchies. 'Dad shoes' (chunky sneakers), logomania's revival, and the oversized silhouette dominated street style. Cottagecore, VSCO girl, and e-girl micro-trends emerged online. Sustainability began entering mainstream fashion conversation.
